s: Central nervous system bacterial infections include meningitis and brain abscesses, where bacteria invade the brain, spinal cord, or their protective membranes.
q: What protects the brain from most bacterial infections?
e: The CNS is normally protected by the blood-brain barrier, which keeps most pathogens out. When bacteria breach this barrier, infections like bacterial meningitis can develop rapidly and become life-threatening. Early antibiotic treatment is critical for survival.

s: Comedone extraction is a dermatological procedure that removes blackheads and whiteheads using a small metal loop tool.
q: What is a comedone?
a: A clogged skin pore (blackhead or whitehead)
e: Comedones form when hair follicles become clogged with dead skin cells and oil. A dermatologist uses a comedone extractor — a small metal tool with a loop — to apply gentle pressure around the clogged pore, pushing out the plug. This is safer than squeezing with fingers, which can cause scarring and infection.

s: A comminuted fracture shatters bone into multiple pieces.
q: What happens in a comminuted fracture?
e: High-energy impacts like car crashes commonly cause comminuted fractures!

s: Complete edentulism means total loss of all teeth.
q: What is complete edentulism?
e: Sharks grow unlimited new teeth, but humans only get two sets for life!

s: Congenital torticollis causes a baby's head to tilt to one side.
q: What does congenital torticollis cause?
e: Babies with torticollis often prefer looking in one direction only!

s: COVID-19 tests detect the SARS-CoV-2 coronavirus in patients.
q: What virus does a COVID-19 test detect?
e: COVID-19 PCR tests can detect just a few virus particles in a sample!

s: Cricoid pressure is a medical technique where a rescuer pushes on the cricoid cartilage ring in the neck to prevent stomach contents from entering the airway.
q: What is the purpose of cricoid pressure?
e: Also called the Sellick maneuver, cricoid pressure compresses the esophagus against the vertebral column. It was traditionally used during emergency intubation to reduce the risk of aspiration. Recent evidence has questioned its effectiveness.

s: Dental pulp exposure occurs when the inner tooth nerve becomes unprotected.
q: What becomes exposed in dental pulp exposure?
e: Tooth enamel is the hardest substance in your body, even harder than bone!

s: A depressed skull fracture pushes bone fragments inward toward the brain.
q: What direction do fragments go in depressed skull fracture?
e: The human skull is made of 22 bones fused together for maximum protection!

s: Diffuse brain injury affects a wide area of brain tissue.
q: How much brain area does diffuse injury affect?
e: Your brain has about 86 billion neurons that can be harmed by diffuse injury!

s: DRD3 encodes the dopamine D3 receptor, which plays a role in mood, motivation, and is a drug target for schizophrenia treatment.
q: What neurotransmitter does the DRD3 receptor respond to?
e: Dopamine receptors in the brain respond to the neurotransmitter dopamine, influencing reward, movement, and emotion. The D3 subtype is concentrated in the limbic system, which processes emotions. Antipsychotic medications often target D3 receptors to help manage symptoms of schizophrenia and addiction.

s: Elastic bandages (like ACE wraps) provide compression to reduce swelling and support injured joints and muscles.
q: What does the 'C' stand for in the RICE protocol?
e: Made of woven elastic fabric, these bandages stretch to apply consistent pressure around an injury. They are a key part of the RICE protocol (Rest, Ice, Compression, Elevation) for sprains and strains. Wrapping too tightly can cut off circulation, so you should always be able to slip a finger underneath.

s: Elbow tendinopathy causes pain from inflamed or degenerated elbow tendons.
q: What causes elbow tendinopathy pain?
e: Tennis elbow got its name in 1883 but most sufferers have never played tennis!

s: Endarteritis is inflammation of the inner lining of an artery.
q: What gets inflamed in endarteritis?
e: If you lined up all your blood vessels end to end, they'd circle Earth twice!

s: Endoscopic stenting places a small tube inside a blocked duct or passage using a camera-guided scope, keeping it open without major surgery.
q: What is an endoscopic stent used for?
e: Stents are tiny mesh or plastic tubes inserted through an endoscope — a flexible camera tube passed through the mouth or other opening. They are used to open blocked bile ducts, esophagus, or airways. The procedure avoids large surgical incisions and patients often go home the same day.

s: Erythropoietin (EPO) is a hormone produced by the kidneys that tells bone marrow to make more red blood cells.
q: Which organ produces erythropoietin (EPO)?
e: When oxygen levels drop — such as at high altitude — the kidneys release more EPO, stimulating red blood cell production. This increases oxygen-carrying capacity. EPO is also used medically to treat anemia and has been infamously misused as a performance-enhancing drug in sports.

s: Esophageal dilation widens a narrowed esophagus for easier swallowing.
q: What does esophageal dilation widen?
e: Your esophagus is about 10 inches long and uses muscles to push food down!

s: An esophagectomy is the surgical removal of all or part of the esophagus, often performed to treat esophageal cancer.
q: What replaces the esophagus after an esophagectomy?
a: The stomach is pulled up into the chest
e: During an esophagectomy, the removed section of esophagus is replaced by pulling the stomach up into the chest or using a section of intestine to create a new food tube. It is one of the most complex operations in surgery, taking 4-8 hours. Patients must relearn how to eat in smaller portions afterward.

s: A fracture dislocation combines a broken bone with a dislocated joint.
q: What two injuries occur in fracture dislocation?
e: The ankle is the most common site for fracture dislocations!

s: Frostbite damages skin and tissue from extreme cold temperatures.
q: What causes frostbite?
e: Frostbite can occur in just 30 minutes at minus 15 degrees Fahrenheit!

s: Hallucinosis is a syndrome of persistent hallucinations while awake.
q: What characterizes hallucinosis?
e: Your brain creates all your experiences, so hallucinations feel completely real!

s: Acquired hand deformities are changes to the hand's shape or function that develop after birth from injury, disease, or overuse.
q: How many bones are in the human hand?
e: Unlike congenital deformities present at birth, acquired hand deformities result from fractures healing incorrectly, arthritis warping joints, tendon injuries, burns, or nerve damage. The hand has 27 bones, 29 joints, and over 120 ligaments, making it vulnerable to many types of deformity.

s: The head tilt chin lift opens a blocked airway in emergencies.
q: What does the head tilt chin lift do?
e: This simple move can save a life by keeping the tongue from blocking air!

s: Heavy metal detoxification removes toxic metals from the body.
q: What does heavy metal detox remove?
e: The word chelation comes from Greek for claw because it grabs metal ions!

s: Hypophosphatemic rickets weakens bones from low phosphate levels.
q: What mineral is deficient in the rickets variant that weakens bones?
e: Rickets was so common in 1600s England it was called the English disease!

s: An insulin coma results from severe low blood sugar from excess insulin.
q: What causes an insulin coma?
e: Your brain uses about 120 grams of glucose daily, more than any other organ!

s: Interleukin 4 (IL-4) is an immune signaling protein that tells white blood cells to fight parasites and is involved in allergic reactions.
q: What type of immune response does IL-4 promote?
e: IL-4 is produced by T-helper cells and promotes the production of IgE antibodies, which are central to allergic responses. It also activates eosinophils and mast cells to fight parasitic infections. Overproduction of IL-4 contributes to asthma, hay fever, and eczema.

s: An intestinal fistula is an abnormal passage between organs.
q: What is an intestinal fistula?
e: The human intestine is about 25 feet long, creating many fistula sites!

s: The IVD gene produces an enzyme needed to break down the amino acid leucine, which is found in protein-rich foods.
q: What amino acid does the IVD enzyme help break down?
e: Isovaleryl-CoA dehydrogenase (IVD) is essential in the metabolic pathway that breaks down leucine from digested proteins. When this enzyme is deficient, toxic compounds build up causing isovaleric acidemia — a metabolic disorder that can make a baby's sweat smell like sweaty feet. Newborn screening can detect it early.

s: KIF11 is a motor protein gene that helps pull chromosomes apart during cell division.
q: What role does the KIF11 protein play in cells?
e: KIF11 encodes a kinesin motor protein that walks along microtubules to separate chromosomes during mitosis. Mutations in KIF11 can cause microcephaly with abnormal brain development. Cancer researchers study it because blocking KIF11 stops tumor cells from dividing.

s: KRIT1 is a gene that maintains blood vessel structure — mutations cause abnormal tangles of blood vessels in the brain called cavernous malformations.
q: What do KRIT1 mutations cause in the brain?
e: KRIT1 protein helps blood vessel cells maintain proper junctions and structure. When mutated, blood vessels form raspberry-like clusters that can leak or bleed, especially in the brain. These cavernous malformations affect about 1 in 200 people, though most never know they have them.

s: The Lamaze technique uses breathing exercises for childbirth pain.
q: What does Lamaze focus on during birth?
e: Dr. Lamaze brought this method from Russia to France in the 1950s!

s: Lead poisoning damages the nervous system through toxic exposure.
q: What system does lead poisoning damage?
e: Ancient Romans unknowingly poisoned themselves with lead water pipes!

s: The Ling Six Sounds test uses six speech sounds — /m/, /ah/, /ee/, /oo/, /sh/, /s/ — to quickly check if a child can hear across all speech frequencies.
q: How many sounds are used in the Ling sounds test?
e: Developed by audiologist Daniel Ling, this simple test covers the full range of speech sounds from low frequency (/m/) to high frequency (/s/). Parents and teachers can perform it daily to check hearing aid or cochlear implant function. If a child cannot detect one of the six sounds, it indicates a gap in their hearing at that frequency.

s: A lumbar puncture collects cerebrospinal fluid from the spine.
q: What does a lumbar puncture collect?
e: Cerebrospinal fluid cushions your brain like a built-in shock absorber!

s: A malunited fracture heals in a faulty or abnormal position.
q: What goes wrong in a malunited fracture?
e: Bones naturally try to heal themselves within about 6 to 8 weeks!

s: A mandibular injury is damage to the lower jaw bone.
q: Which bone is affected in mandibular injury?
e: Your jaw can exert up to 200 pounds of force when biting down!

s: A maxillary fracture is a break in the upper jaw bone.
q: Which bone breaks in a maxillary fracture?
e: The maxilla is actually two bones fused together at the center of your face!

s: MCM6 is the gene that determines whether adults can digest milk — a mutation near it lets some people produce lactase into adulthood.
q: What does a mutation near MCM6 allow some adults to do?
e: Most mammals stop producing lactase (the milk-digesting enzyme) after weaning. A regulatory region within the MCM6 gene controls lactase persistence in humans. About 35% of the world's population carries mutations that keep lactase production going, allowing them to drink milk as adults without digestive problems.

s: Microcephaly is a condition where a baby's head is significantly smaller than expected, often affecting brain development.
q: What characterizes microcephaly?
e: Primary microcephaly is present at birth and can be caused by genetic mutations. The brain does not grow to its normal size, which may lead to developmental delays. Some forms are inherited in an autosomal recessive pattern.

s: Mouth abnormalities are congenital defects in mouth structures.
q: When do mouth abnormalities develop?
e: About 1 in 700 babies is born with a cleft lip or palate worldwide!

s: MT-TH is a transfer RNA gene located in mitochondrial DNA, not in the cell nucleus like most genes.
q: Where is the MT-TH gene located?
e: MT-TH encodes a transfer RNA for the amino acid histidine within the mitochondria. Mitochondria have their own small circular genome inherited only from the mother. Mutations in MT-TH can cause hearing loss and other mitochondrial disorders.

s: Nebulin (NEB) is one of the largest proteins in the human body, stretching along muscle fibers to regulate their length.
q: What is the function of the nebulin protein in muscles?
e: Nebulin acts as a molecular ruler inside muscle cells, determining how long the thin filaments (actin) should be. At over 6,000 amino acids long, it is a giant protein. Mutations in the NEB gene are the most common cause of nemaline myopathy, a muscle weakness disorder.

s: Nerve degeneration causes loss of function in nerve fibers.
q: What happens during nerve degeneration?
e: Nerve signals can travel up to 268 mph, faster than a race car!

s: Occludin is a protein that forms the tight seals between cells, preventing fluids from leaking through tissue barriers.
q: What structure does occludin help form between cells?
e: Occludin is a key component of tight junctions — the molecular zippers that seal neighboring cells together. These junctions are critical in the gut lining, blood-brain barrier, and skin. When occludin is disrupted, barriers become leaky, contributing to conditions like inflammatory bowel disease.

s: An oesophagotomy is a surgical incision into the oesophagus, often performed to remove a foreign body that is stuck.
q: What is an oesophagotomy?
e: The oesophagus (esophagus) is the muscular tube connecting the throat to the stomach. When an object becomes lodged and cannot be removed endoscopically, surgeons may cut into the oesophagus directly. The procedure requires careful closure to prevent leaking.

s: Oneirism is a state of abnormal consciousness where dream-like experiences and hallucinations occur while a person is awake.
q: What is oneirism?
d: Complete inability to enter REM sleep
e: Unlike normal dreaming during sleep, oneirism blurs the boundary between waking and dreaming states. It can be triggered by fever, certain medications, or neurological conditions. The term comes from the Greek word 'oneiros' meaning dream.

s: Optic nerve injury can cause vision loss from head or face trauma.
q: What can optic nerve injury cause?
e: Each optic nerve contains about one million nerve fibers carrying vision data!

s: Oral hemorrhage is bleeding from blood vessels inside the mouth.
q: What is oral hemorrhage?
e: The mouth has more blood vessels per area than almost any other body part!

s: Ototoxicity means a substance is toxic to the ear and hearing.
q: What does ototoxicity damage?
e: The tiny hair cells in your inner ear cannot regrow once damaged!
